sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Michelle King, a 36-year-old female, presents with a 24-year history of acid reflux, which has worsened over the past few years.
She reports taking 40 mg of omeprazole twice daily, but her symptoms persist.

She describes the sensation of burning in her chest and throat, particularly after consuming certain foods and beverages, including spicy foods, carbonated drinks, citrus products, and tomato-based products.
She denies difficulty swallowing, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, changes in bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, or rectal bleeding.
She has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.
Review of systems: The patient reports a stable weight, although she has had a decreased appetite for the past 2 months due to work-related stress.
Current medications: Omeprazole 40 mg twice daily.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: A well-healed abdominal surgical scar is noted, which the patient attributes to a surgery she underwent at 1 month old for intestinal twisting.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I suspect that the patient likely has gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with an underlying functional etiology.
Problem list: GERD; suspected; confirmed; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I prescribed Nexium 40 mg twice daily to replace her current omeprazole regimen.
I recommend that she avoid trigger foods, including spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus products, and tomato-based products, and work on managing stress and anxiety.
I also recommend an upper endoscopy (EGD) to investigate potential causes of her GERD, including ulcers, precancerous, or cancerous conditions.
The procedure will involve sedation and a small camera passed through her mouth to examine her esophagus, stomach, and small intestines.
I will send a prescription for Nexium to her pharmacy on file and schedule the EGD at the checkout desk.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in the office for follow-up after the EGD to discuss the results and assess her progress with the dietary changes and medication adjustments.

If the EGD is normal and her symptoms persist, we can consider a trial of Protonix and obtain a pH impedance study to rule out abnormal acid reflux exposure and determine the etiology of her symptoms.
